definition: A proposal by one party expressing willingness to enter into a contract on specific, definite terms. An offer lapses when rejected, when a counteroffer is made, or after a reasonable time. A bid or estimate is not automatically an offer.
An unconditional agreement to all terms of an offer exactly as made. Any modification of terms creates a counteroffer, voids the original offer, and starts a new negotiation.

Something of legal value exchanged by each party to a contract — typically a promise to perform work or a promise to pay. A promise to do something already legally required does not constitute valid consideration.

Failure by one party to perform contractual obligations without legal excuse. A material breach entitles the non-breaching party to treat the contract as terminated and sue for compensatory damages. A minor breach allows damages but not termination.

A written, signed amendment to an existing construction contract that documents a change in scope, price, or schedule. When the original contract is written, change orders must also be in writing and signed by both parties before the changed work begins.

A contractual clause specifying a predetermined amount — often per day — owed if a party fails to perform on time. Enforceable only when the specified amount is a reasonable pre-estimate of actual damages at the time of contracting, not a penalty.

A percentage — typically 5 to 10 percent — of each progress payment withheld by the project owner until substantial completion. Retainage incentivizes the contractor to complete punch-list items and provides a fund to cover latent defects.

An active Oregon Construction Contractors Board license that a contractor must hold before performing compensated construction work or advertising as a contractor. The license number must appear in written contracts and specified advertising media.

The individual designated on a contractor's CCB license application who is responsible for the qualifying examination, maintaining required insurance and bond, and ensuring the business complies with Oregon contractor law under ORS Chapter 701.

A CCB-required disclosure that must be delivered to a residential client before work begins on a project subject to ORS 701.305. OAR 812-012-0130 specifies the required delivery timing and requires proof-of-delivery to be retained.
An Oregon code volume adopted for a specific construction discipline (for example, structural or residential), with current edition status and amendments published by BCD.
Official authorizations for covered construction work under Oregon's building code framework; CCB guidance emphasizes that contractors or companies must ensure required permits are obtained.
A local jurisdiction program that administers building inspection and related permitting functions and renews under OAR 918-020 reporting cycles described by BCD.
A statutory cancellation right that allows a buyer or owner to cancel within a legally defined period when the governing Oregon law grants that right for the transaction.
relatedTerms.definition: Under ORS 701.305, a written residential contract is required when the aggregate contract price exceeds $2,000; if the price later crosses that amount, the contractor must deliver or mail the written contract within five days after knowing or having reason to know the threshold was exceeded.

For covered residential work under ORS 701.305, written-contract duties apply when the aggregate contract price exceeds $2,000, including a five-day delivery duty if the project crosses the threshold after work starts.

A statutory cancellation right allowing an owner to cancel an initial written residential contract until midnight of the next business day under ORS 701.310, with listed exceptions; qualifying home solicitation sales follow separate ORS 83 timelines.

A transaction category regulated by ORS chapter 83 that requires specific written agreement content and cancellation-right notices, generally with a three-business-day cancellation period for qualifying sales.

A transaction governed by ORS 83.720 and ORS 83.730 that generally gives a buyer until midnight of the third business day to cancel and requires a signed written agreement with prescribed cancellation notice content.
A person capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in surroundings or working conditions and authorized to take prompt corrective measures under applicable Oregon safety rules.
Oregon construction requirements designed to prevent fall injuries, including Oregon-specific duty provisions and employee training obligations for workers exposed to fall hazards.
Protective equipment selected through hazard assessment and maintained in sanitary, reliable condition, with worker training on use, limits, and care required by Oregon construction safety rules.
Oregon trenching and excavation requirements covering safe egress, competent-person inspections, and cave-in protective systems when rule triggers are met.
Oregon stairway and ladder rules requiring safe ladder condition and setup, including removal of defective ladders from service.
Covered renovation work in pre-1978 target housing or child-occupied facilities that requires Oregon lead training/certification pathways and contractor licensing compliance.
Required methods in Oregon lead-renovation programs that reduce lead exposure risk during covered work in older housing and child-occupied settings.
Oregon DEQ-administered asbestos compliance duties, including requirements codified in OAR chapter 340, division 248 and related contractor/business prerequisites.
Oregon construction safety compliance framework combining ORS chapter 654 duties with Oregon OSHA Division 3 operational rules.
Exam domain focused on recognizing Oregon lead and asbestos compliance triggers, agency authority, and pre-work licensing or certification obligations.
